**Tier 1 Audit — The 6 To Fix Marketing Audit**

**What it is and why it exists**

Tier 1 is the diagnostic wedge in the productized three-tier ladder you developed for Strategic Glue. Sized at **$3,500 fixed fee, 2-week delivery**, it's calibrated to do four jobs at once:

* **Frictionless entry** — low enough that an owner who'd balk at $15K can say yes
* **Credentialing** — the engagement itself proves the methodology before you ask for a larger commitment
* **Qualification filter** — disqualifies tire-kickers and surfaces real Tier 2/3 prospects
* **Conversion engine** — every Tier 2/3 engagement begins with a Tier 1; the readout is the close

Target buyer: owner-operated business, **$2M–$10M revenue**, growth/succession/exit motivation, some existing marketing activity but unsystematized. Sub-$1M is too early. Above ~$15M with internal team routes to direct Tier 3 conversation. Conversion target: 60–70% of audits progressing to Tier 2 within 45 days of readout.

**The Six Deliverables**

**1. 6 To Fix Scorecard**

**Format:** Two-page scored PDF, dashboard

**Content:** Numerical score 0–10 in each of the six strategic areas (Brand, Customer, Offering, Communications, Sales, Management). Total Marketing Maturity Score out of 60. Each area score reflects both maturity of activity and the systems-level properties — documented, repeatable, measurable, owner-independent.

**Purpose:** The quantitative anchor. A measurable baseline and a future re-score target.

**2. Gap Analysis Report**

**Format:** 8–12 page narrative PDF, dashboard

**Content:** For each of the six areas: current state observations (evidence-based), gaps identified, and impact if not addressed. Each gap is explicitly framed as activity vs. system — distinguishing "the work isn't happening" from "the work is happening but isn't a system."

**Purpose:** Qualitative depth. The story the score quantifies. The reference document the owner returns to.

**3. Value Driver Assessment**

**Format:** Single-page dashboard PDF, dashboard

**Content:** Rates the client's current state against the **6 Value Drivers Marketing Owns** (the bridge between marketing performance and enterprise value). Includes industry benchmark data showing where peer businesses score.

**Purpose:** Connects the marketing diagnosis to enterprise value — the language the exit/succession-minded owner already speaks.

**4. Prioritized 90-Day Roadmap**

**Format:** PDF + simple action tracker, dashboard

**Content:** Top 6–10 priorities ranked by value impact and execution difficulty. Each priority is tagged human-effort vs. AI-automatable, and identifies whether the action builds *activity* (do more of the work) or builds *systems* (make the work repeatable, measurable, documented, owner-independent). **Purpose:** Converts diagnosis into action. Makes the systems-vs-activity distinction operational.

**5. AI-Readiness Assessment**

**Format:** Multi-section PDF — the conversion accelerator, dashboard

**Content:**

* **Current State Inventory** — every marketing activity categorized as AI-native, AI-assisted, or human-essential
* **Automation Opportunity Score** — % of marketing work that could be AI-executed in Tier 3 (typical for ICP: 55–75%)
* **Labor Savings Projection** — $ to staff equivalent in-house team (typical for $3M–$8M businesses: $180K–$320K/year loaded)
* **Tier 3 Economic Comparison** — three-year side-by-side: Do Nothing vs. Hire In-House vs. Tier 3 fCMO+ AI
* **AI Implementation Roadmap** — sequencing for which AI capabilities go live first, second, third

**Purpose:** The other deliverables convince the client they have a problem. This one convinces the client that Tier 3 is the economically rational answer at their size — $42–66K/year vs. $250K+ for an equivalent human team.

**6. 90-Minute Readout**

**Format:** Live presentation; recording delivered, dashboard

**Content:** Walks through all five prior deliverables in a single narrative arc — current state (Scorecard + Gaps) → enterprise-value impact (Value Drivers) → path forward (Roadmap + AI Readiness). Closes with one of three concrete recommendations: self-execute, proceed to Tier 2, or proceed through Tier 2 to Tier 3.

**Purpose:** The human conversion moment. The audit is a paid sales meeting; this is where the next-tier engagement is sold — not as a pitch, but as the obvious next step.

**Note on version:** The current v2 spec (in the launch deck source) splits the Scorecard into two deliverables by separating out a **Systems Maturity Score** (0–20 across Documented / Repeatable / Measurable / Owner-Independent), which makes it a seven-deliverable package. The six-deliverable version above treats systems-maturity as a dimension *inside* the Scorecard. Worth deciding which framing you want as the canonical client-facing one.

**How much of this can be automated?** 

The audit is the highest-leverage part of the entire offering for AI automation — it's a structured intake → analysis → deliverable pipeline, and every stage benefits. Here's the honest breakdown by deliverable, holding to the principle of distinguishing what AI actually does well from what still needs a human.

**The infrastructure layer (build this once)**

The single biggest unlock is a **Claude Skill library** that encodes your methodology, so every audit scores against the same rubric. One-time build effort: **25–35 hours**. Skills to build:

* **6tofix-scorecard-rubric** — scoring criteria for each of the six areas, both activity and systems dimensions
* **systems-maturity-scoring** — the 4-dimension Doc/Repeat/Measure/Owner-Indep rubric
* **gap-analysis-template** — narrative structure, evidence requirements, activity-vs-system framing
* **value-driver-rating** — scoring criteria against the 6 Value Drivers Marketing Owns
* **ai-readiness-projection** — the capability matrix and labor-cost calculation logic

Without these Skills, Claude produces good but inconsistent output across audits. With them, every audit is reproducible and defensible — that's the productized integrity that makes the offer scalable.

Plus a **Claude Project per audit client** holding intake materials, transcripts, competitive scans, and in-progress deliverables.

**Pre-deliverable automation (the upstream pipeline)**

This is where you save the most hours, because intake and inventory are the time-killers in any audit:

* **Marketing Asset Inventory automation** — a script (Claude Code / Cowork) that crawls the client's website, pulls their public ad output, samples their social presence, scans email archives, and produces a "current state" inventory. **Saves 4–6 hours per audit.**
* **Competitive Intelligence scan** — Perplexity or Claude with web search runs a structured prompt against 3–5 named competitors covering positioning, content output, channel presence, review sentiment, hiring patterns. **30 minutes vs. 10–15 hours of consulting time, with comparable depth.**
* **Industry Benchmark sourcing** — Apollo or Clay for peer benchmarks on spend-as-percent-of-revenue, channel mix, conversion rates by vertical and revenue band.
* **Interview transcription** — Otter or AssemblyAI on every intake and stakeholder call, feeding directly into the Project knowledge base.
* **Orchestration** — n8n or Zapier triggers asset-inventory automation on engagement kickoff and delivers outputs to the Project automatically. Same orchestration handles delivery handoffs.

**Honest limits — where AI does *not* add value here**

Worth being explicit, since this maps to the principle in your existing AI execution matrix:

* **The intake call** — the 90-minute owner conversation is where most of the real signal comes from. AI transcribes and structures, but the conversation has to be human.
* **The "what would actually work for *this* owner" judgment** — sequencing the roadmap, calling the Tier 2 vs. Tier 3 recommendation, anticipating which gaps the owner will defend. AI scaffolds; you decide.
* **The readout itself** — the audit is a paid sales meeting and the readout is the close. Automating the presentation would gut the conversion logic of the entire offer.

**What the automation gets you economically**

For a 20–25 hour senior consulting effort at $3,500, the unit economics today are reasonable but not great. With the Skill library built and the upstream pipeline running, **realistic effective time per audit drops to 8–12 hours of senior time** — the rest being review, judgment calls, the intake call, and the readout. That moves the audit from a credentialing loss-leader into a genuinely profitable standalone product, while preserving the 60–70% Tier 2/3 conversion logic.

The Skill library is also the asset that lets you eventually run this with Ben as the operator and you reviewing — which is the whole point of building it as productized IP rather than bespoke consulting.

**Added *Documented Strategy* as Another Deliverable:** 

* **Systems Maturity → Documented** asks whether the *execution* is documented — could a new team member do the work tomorrow without the owner explaining each step? (Process documentation: SOPs, checklists, playbooks.)
* **Documented Strategy** asks whether the *thinking* is documented — could a new team member tell you *why* the work is structured this way without the owner explaining? (Strategic documentation: positioning, ICP, channel strategy — the "why" behind the work.)
* **6 To Fix Scorecard** measures *what* you do — the six strategic areas of marketing work (Brand, Customer, Offering, Communications, Sales, Management)
* **Systems Maturity Score** measures *how* you do it — whether the work is *Documented*, Repeatable, Measurable, Owner-Independent

A client can score 8/10 on Brand activity (lots of branding work happening) but 4/20 on Systems Maturity (none of it documented or repeatable). That's a fundamentally different problem than scoring 4/10 on Brand activity. Embedding the systems dimension inside the 6 To Fix Scorecard hides this.

Three reasons to break it out *Documented Strategy* as its own deliverable:

1. **It's the bridge to your value-creation story.** Documented, repeatable, owner-independent processes *are* the value drivers. Surfacing systems maturity as its own score makes that connection explicit — and lands in the language exit/succession-minded owners already speak.
2. **It sets up the AI-Readiness Assessment.** The logical chain "you have gaps → the gaps are systems-shaped → AI is the most efficient way to build systems → here's the labor-cost differential" works much better when each step has its own deliverable. Without a Systems Maturity Score, the leap from Gap Analysis to AI-Readiness is harder to land.
3. **It opens a different sales conversation.** "You're doing the work, but the work isn't a system" is a more sophisticated diagnosis than "you're not doing enough work." The first lands as a sophisticated observation; the second sounds like a generic agency pitch.

The 6 To Fix Scorecard focuses purely on activity-and-quality across the six strategy areas; Systems Maturity Score its own deliverable positioned right after the Gap Analysis so the narrative arc reads *current state → diagnosis → systems lens → value impact → path forward*. Seven named deliverables at $3,500 reinforces value perception, not the opposite.

A client can have one without the other; in practice, owner-operated $2M–$10M businesses often have neither.

**What "Current" means per area**

The rubric needs explicit anchors per area, because documented strategy looks different in Brand than in Sales. Working definitions:

**Brand — Current requires:** positioning statement, target audience definition, brand voice and tone guide, differentiation statement / brand promise. *Anchor: at least 3 of 4 documented and updated within 12 months.*

**Customer — Current requires:** ICP definition (firmographic and behavioral), at least one fully developed persona, documented acquisition strategy (channels, motion, CAC targets), documented retention strategy (cadence, programs, retention targets), customer journey map. *Anchor: ICP + at least one persona + both acquisition and retention strategies, current.*

**Offering — Current requires:** value proposition, USP, primary and secondary offering definitions, pricing strategy and rationale, competitive positioning. *Anchor: value prop, USP, and competitive positioning documented and current.*

**Communications — Current requires:** channel strategy (which channels, why, what role each plays), messaging framework (pillars, proof points, calls to action), content cadence and editorial direction, campaign architecture. *Anchor: channel strategy + messaging framework documented and current.*

**Sales — Current requires:** pipeline stages and qualification criteria, sales process documented, objection handling, marketing-to-sales handoff defined. *Anchor: pipeline + qualification + handoff documented and current.*

**Management — Current requires:** KPI and metric framework, reporting cadence and dashboard, marketing budget, vendor and team structure. *Anchor: KPI framework + reporting cadence documented and current.*

**Partial** = some elements present but missing critical pieces, scattered without a through-line, or stale (>12 months without update).

**None** = no documented strategy for this area exists.

**Why this is also a conversion lever:**

The Documented Strategy Coverage score gives the readout a number-driven path to the right tier recommendation:

* **0–2 of 6 documented** → Tier 2 is the obvious next step. The Tier 2 Playbook *is* documented strategy across all six areas. Direct match.
* **3–4 of 6 documented** → Targeted Tier 2 to fill specific gaps or self-execute with the roadmap if the owner has the discipline and bandwidth.
* **5–6 of 6 documented** → Human judgement call – poor strategy could reinforce Tier 2 or potentially skip Tier 2 and go directly to Tier 3 ; focus the Tier 3 conversation on execution capacity, or recommend a refresh-and-execute path.

That sharpens what was previously a softer judgment-based recommendation at the readout. The Documented Strategy Coverage number does much of the work for you.

**Automation by deliverable**

| **Deliverable** | **Automation level** | **What AI does** | **What stays human** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **1. 6 To Fix Scorecard** | High (~80%) | Claude with scorecard-rubric Skill scores against intake transcript + asset inventory + materials review | Final score sanity check; calibration across the audit book |
| **2. Systems Maturity Score** | TBD | Single-page scored PDF, dashboard. Independent four-dimension score (Documented / Repeatable / Measurable / Owner-Independent), 0–5 each, total 0–20. Distinct from Documented Strategy — measures execution, not thinking. | TBD |
| **3. Gap Analysis Report** | High (~75%) | Drafts the narrative for each area using gap-analysis-template Skill, with evidence pulled from the Project | Judgment-based observations from interviews; tone and emphasis; the line that lands with this owner |
| **4. Value Driver Assessment** | High (~85%) | Rates each driver from intake data; pulls industry benchmarks via Perplexity / web search | Cross-check against actual conversations; flag where benchmark data is thin |
| **5. Prioritized 90-Day Roadmap** | Partial (~60%) | Generates initial roadmap from Scorecard + Gap Analysis; tags each item human-vs-AI | Final prioritization and sequencing — this is judgment, especially the political read of what the owner can actually push through |
| **6. AI-Readiness Assessment** | Very high (~90%) | Capability matrix runs as a Skill; labor calculation is pure logic; asset inventory feeds in automatically | Final labor-cost assumptions for that owner's market; sense-check the Tier 3 comparison |
| **7. 90-Minute Readout** | Low (~25%) | Generates slides, talking points, anticipated objections, recording transcript | The presentation itself, Q&A, the recommendation, the close — this is the conversion moment, and it's human-essential |

**Seven-Deliverable Version for the Skill Library Spec:** 

**Skill Library Specification**

Anthropic Claude Skills are reusable methodology modules — each one is a SKILL.md plus supporting reference files (rubrics, templates, calibration examples) that Claude loads when the task matches. The point is consistency: every audit Strategic Glue delivers should score against the same rubric, follow the same narrative structure, and produce comparable outputs across the audit book.

A useful distinction before the skills themselves:

* **Skills are reusable IP** — methodology that applies across every client. Built once, version-controlled, improved over time.
* **Project knowledge is per-client** — interview transcripts, asset inventory, materials packet, in-progress deliverables. Lives inside each client's Claude Project.
* **Code/scripts are operational utilities** — the asset-inventory crawler, the competitive scan harness. Live in a shared repo, called by Claude or by n8n.

Eight skills covering the audit. The first five are the core scoring/diagnostic IP; the last three are upstream and downstream support.

**1. intake-interview-guide**

**Purpose:** Drives the 90-minute owner conversation and the three stakeholder interviews. Ensures every audit collects the same signal.

**Contents:** Six-area structured question bank, systems-level questions ("what runs without you," "what's documented," "what couldn't you hand off tomorrow"), value-driver probes, follow-up logic by answer type.

**Inputs:** Client name, industry, revenue band.

**Outputs:** A pre-call interview guide tailored to the client; post-call, a structured summary of answers mapped to the six areas + four systems dimensions + six value drivers.

**Maintenance:** Quarterly review based on which questions actually surfaced signal vs. which were dead weight. Version the question bank.

**2. 6tofix-scorecard-rubric**

**Purpose:** Scores each of the six strategic areas 0–10 against a defined rubric. Same rubric every audit.

**Contents:** Six rubrics (one per area), each with anchor descriptions for scores 0, 3, 5, 7, 10. Calibration set of 5–10 anonymized prior audits with their scores and the reasoning.

**Inputs:** Project knowledge (interview transcripts, materials review, asset inventory).

**Outputs:** Six area scores 0–10 with one-paragraph justification each, citing specific evidence from the Project. Total Marketing Maturity Score out of 60.

**Maintenance:** Re-anchor calibration set every 10 audits. The first ~10 audits will reveal where the rubric is too generous or too harsh.

**3. systems-maturity-scoring**

**Purpose:** Scores the marketing operation against the four systems dimensions. Independent of the 6 To Fix Scorecard.

**Contents:** Four sub-rubrics (Documented, Repeatable, Measurable, Owner-Independent), each 0–5, total 0–20. Anchor descriptions per dimension. Calibration set of prior audits.

**Inputs:** Project knowledge — particularly the documentation question answers, the "what runs without you" responses, and the materials review.

**Outputs:** Four-dimension scores 0–5, total Systems Maturity Score 0–20, narrative for each dimension explaining the score.

**Maintenance:** Watch for systematic over- or under-scoring on Owner-Independent — this is the dimension owners most consistently misjudge themselves on.

**4. gap-analysis-template**

**Purpose:** Produces the 8–12 page narrative Gap Analysis Report.

**Contents:** Section structure for each of the six areas (current state observation → gap → impact if not addressed → activity-vs-system framing). Style guide for tone and evidence requirements (every claim cited from Project knowledge). Forbidden patterns (no generic "you should do more content"-type filler).

**Inputs:** Scorecard outputs, Systems Maturity outputs, Project knowledge.

**Outputs:** Draft Gap Analysis Report. Human edits add the judgment-based observations and the line that lands with this specific owner.

**Maintenance:** Add new patterns to the forbidden list as you catch them in drafts. The skill should get sharper at avoiding generic language over time.

**5. value-driver-rating**

**Purpose:** Scores the client against the 6 Value Drivers Marketing Owns and produces the dashboard.

**Contents:** Six driver definitions, scoring criteria 0–5 per driver, anchor examples, peer benchmark logic.

**Inputs:** Scorecard, Systems Maturity, materials review, peer benchmarks (sourced separately via Perplexity / Apollo).

**Outputs:** Single-page Value Driver Assessment dashboard with scores, peer benchmark comparison, and a narrative tying marketing maturity to enterprise value.

**Maintenance:** Refresh peer benchmark data quarterly.

**6. ai-readiness-projection**

**Purpose:** Produces the AI-Readiness Assessment — capability matrix, automation opportunity score, labor-savings projection, three-way economic comparison, AI implementation roadmap.

**Contents:** The full AI capability matrix you've already developed (research types, copywriting/image generation, Acquisition/Retention sub-categories, etc.). Labor-cost calculation logic with adjustable inputs for market and benefits load. Three-year economic comparison template. AI implementation sequencing logic.

**Inputs:** Project knowledge — specifically the marketing activity inventory and the MarTech stack.

**Outputs:** Multi-section AI-Readiness Assessment PDF.

**Maintenance:** This is the highest-maintenance skill because the AI capability landscape shifts. Quarterly review of capability classifications.

**7. roadmap-prioritization**

**Purpose:** Drafts the Prioritized 90-Day Roadmap from the Gap Analysis and Scorecard.

**Contents:** Prioritization rubric (value impact × execution difficulty), human-vs-AI tagging logic, activity-vs-system tagging logic, sequencing rules (quick wins first, systems-building items second, complex orchestration third).

**Inputs:** All prior deliverable outputs.

**Outputs:** Draft top 6–10 priorities with full tagging. Final prioritization is human — the skill scaffolds, you decide.

**Maintenance:** Review which roadmap items clients actually executed vs. ignored, and recalibrate the rubric.

**8. competitive-intel-scan**

**Purpose:** Structures the 30-minute competitive intelligence pull.

**Contents:** Standard prompt template for Perplexity / Claude with web search, covering positioning, content output, channel presence, review sentiment, hiring patterns. Output structure that feeds directly into the Gap Analysis (Brand and Communications areas particularly).

**Inputs:** 3–5 named competitors from intake.

**Outputs:** Structured competitive scan, ~2–3 pages.

**Maintenance:** Lower than the others; the prompt template is fairly stable.

**Build sequence and effort:**

| **Phase** | **Skills** | **Effort** | **When** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Phase 1 (foundation)** | 6tofix-scorecard-rubric, systems-maturity-scoring, gap-analysis-template | 12–15 hrs | Build first; everything else depends on these |
| **Phase 2 (differentiation)** | value-driver-rating, ai-readiness-projection | 8–10 hrs | The deliverables that distinguish your audit from a generic marketing audit |
| **Phase 3 (efficiency)** | intake-interview-guide, roadmap-prioritization, competitive-intel-scan | 6–8 hrs | Save time on every audit but not blockers for delivery |

**Total one-time effort: 26–33 hours.** Recouped on roughly the third audit through time savings. Real return is consistency and defensibility — every audit scores against the same rubric, which is the productized integrity that lets you eventually run audits with Ben as the operator and you on review. Here's the pipeline diagram showing how intake, methodology, and deliverables fit together — with color coding for human-led vs. AI-automated work: The flow reads left to right: human-led inputs (interviews, materials packet) and AI-automated inputs (asset crawl, competitive scan, benchmarks) all feed the per-client Project. The Skills Library applies your methodology consistently across every audit, producing the six artifact deliverables. The 90-minute Readout sits separately on purpose — it's the seventh deliverable in delivery sequence but it's the human conversion moment, not an artifact, and the diagram should make that distinction visible.

Two things worth noting:

**The Skills Library is the asset that makes this system defensible.** Without it, the same inputs would produce different outputs depending on who's running the audit — that's bespoke consulting, not a productized service. With it, every audit is reproducible against the same rubrics, which is what eventually lets you delegate audit operation to Ben while preserving quality control on review.

**The Project layer is what makes Skills work.** Skills are reusable methodology; Projects are per-client knowledge. The skills don't score anything in the abstract — they score against the specific evidence in this client's Project. That's why both have to exist as separate layers.

**Downstream impacts to wire up**

This addition creates a few ripples through the rest of the system:

* **Skill update** — 6tofix-scorecard-rubric adds the per-area documented-strategy assessment with the anchor definitions above. Add a supporting reference file documented-strategy-elements.md listing the elements per area for consistent scoring.
* **Gap Analysis Report** — for any area scoring None or Partial, the report explicitly calls out missing strategy documentation as a top-tier gap. The gap-analysis-template skill should auto-flag this so it's never quietly omitted.
* **90-Day Roadmap** — Documented Strategy gaps auto-prioritize as systems-building items, since strategy documentation is a prerequisite for the rest of the work being a system rather than ad-hoc activity.
* **Tier 2 positioning** — your Tier 2 messaging gets sharper because you can now make the direct claim: *"Tier 2 produces documented strategy across all 6 areas in 30 days."* That's the literal answer to a 0/6 or 1/6 Tier 1 score.

**Dashboard Mockup**

**The architectural principle: one data model, two presentations**

Each Tier 1 audit should produce a single structured JSON output as its real artifact. That JSON drives both the interactive dashboard and the PDF. Same numbers, same evidence, same gap statements — different containers.

The dashboard is the *living* version: client logs in, drills into any area, sees roadmap progress, eventually re-scores. The PDF is the *archived snapshot*: timestamped, shareable with advisors, lender, board, prospective acquirer.

This separation matters for three reasons:

* **Consistency** — eliminates the risk of dashboard and PDF drifting apart as you iterate
* **Versioning** — each re-score creates a new JSON; deltas become visualizable in the dashboard automatically
* **Build economics** — you build the data model once and render it twice, instead of maintaining two parallel pipelines

The Skills Library produces the JSON. The PDF generator and the dashboard both consume it.

Here's a wireframe of the dashboard structure based on Claude discussion:

![A screenshot of a computer

AI-generated content may be incorrect.](data:image/png;base64,)

The Documented Strategy pill is the most semantically loaded element on each card, so it gets the only color. Activity Score is the visual weight (largest type), and the gap callout sits at the bottom in muted text — the eye reads name → strategy state → score → gap, which is the order an owner naturally processes the information.

The headline strip resolves the "what should I do" question before the owner even scans the cards. The tier recommendation pill is generated directly from the Documented Strategy Coverage rule (1/6 → Tier 2). That's the conversion mechanic working through the data structure, not through the readout.

The bottom row makes the AI-Readiness widget do something the PDF can't do as effectively: surface the labor-savings number prominently as a *destination*, not buried mid-document. The "$157K net annual benefit" line is the single most persuasive number in the entire audit, and it deserves visual primacy on the dashboard.

Two construction notes worth flagging while this is fresh:

The data schema needed to drive this dashboard is straightforward — roughly six area objects (each with activity\_score, documented\_strategy enum, documented\_strategy\_elements checklist, top\_gap, gaps[]), six value driver scores, four systems maturity sub-scores, an AI-readiness object with the four numbers, and engagement metadata. That's the JSON schema to lock in first. Both the dashboard and the PDF generator consume it; the Skills Library produces it.

The drill-down panel for any selected area card is a separate component you build once. It takes one area object as a prop and renders the activity sub-criteria, the documented-strategy element checklist (the big payoff — owner sees exactly what's missing), the gap narrative, and the roadmap items tagged to this area. Building this component well is what makes the dashboard useful beyond a pretty score display.

**Construction approach** 

Build this in three phases, deferring complexity as long as possible:

**Phase 1 — MVP (~2-3 weeks of focused build)** Single-client portal per audit. Hardcoded routing: each client gets a unique URL like portal.strategicglue.com/c/[client-slug]. JSON file per client stored in a simple backend (Supabase, Airtable, or even GitHub-as-CMS for the very first version). Auth via magic link or simple password. React + Tailwind for the UI. The whole thing reads one JSON file and renders the five zones.

This MVP can credibly be built in **Lovable.ai or a similar AI-native build tool** since the structure is well-defined and the components are standard. Worth doing the design (wireframe + JSON schema) by hand before letting the AI builder code it, so the data model is locked.

**Phase 2 — Multi-client + audit history (~1-2 months)** Real auth (Clerk or Supabase Auth), multi-tenant data, audit versioning so re-scores create new snapshots and deltas render automatically. Roadmap items become interactive — client can mark complete, add notes, attach evidence. Comments/threads on each gap. This is where the dashboard starts becoming the central UI of the engagement, not just an audit artifact.

**Phase 3 — The subscription product UI (~3-6 months)** The dashboard expands beyond Tier 1 to become the platform for Tier 2 and Tier 3 engagements: Playbook content lives here, monthly sprint calendars, content calendars, KPI dashboards, AI execution layer status. The Tier 1 Audit dashboard is the seed; the full client portal is what it grows into.

**Construction principles to lock in now**

A few decisions made now save real rework later:

* **JSON schema first, before any UI code.** The Skills Library should produce JSON in a defined schema; the dashboard and PDF both consume it. Lock the schema before you build either renderer.
* **Component library, not bespoke screens.** The same area card component appears six times in the Scorecard view; the same drill-down panel pattern serves all six. Build the components once, render them with data.
* **Print-first thinking.** The PDF is generated from the same JSON. If the dashboard data structure can't render to a clean PDF, the data structure is wrong.
* **No native dashboard charts library yet.** Recharts or similar is plenty for Phase 1. Don't reach for D3 or anything heavier until you've shipped to three real clients.
* **Authentication is not the interesting problem.** Use a managed provider (Clerk, Supabase Auth, Auth.js). Spending three weeks on auth is the most common Phase 1 mistake.
